content.problemStatement:
Sereja likes to hang around trees. A tree is an undirected graph on **N** vertices with **N-1** edges and no cycles. Sereja has his own peculiar way of comparing two trees. To describe it, let's start with the way Sereja stores a tree. For every tree, Sereja has a value **V** — the root of the tree, and for every vertex **i**, he has an ordered list **Q[i]** with **L[i]** elements — **Q[i][1], Q[i][2], ..., Q[i][L[i]]** which are children of the vertex **i**. Sereja assumes two trees to be equal if their roots are the same and for every **i**, the ordered list **Q[i]** is the same in both the trees that Sereja compares.


So if Sereja has tree#1 given as **[V=1, Q[1]=[2, 3], Q[2]=[], Q[3]=[]]** and tree#2 given as **[V=1, Q[1]=[3, 2], Q[2]=[], Q[3]=[]]**, they will be considered different because **Q[1]** in the first tree is not equal to **Q[1]** in the second tree.


For any vertex **i**, Sereja calls number of vertices adjacent to it as **E[i]**.


Given an array **C** of **N** elements, Let **f(C)** be the number of different trees (in Sereja's representation) such that there exists a permutation **P[1], P[2], ... , P[N]** so that **E[P[1]]=C[1], E[P[2]]= C[2], ... , E[P[N]]=C[N]**.


Sereja gives you the array **C**. You have to compute the number **f(C)** modulo **1000000007 (109+7)**.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer N, the number of vertices in the tree. The second line contains N space-separated integers, representing the array C.
content.outputFormat: The number of different trees (in Sereja's representation) such that there exists a permutation P[1], P[2], ... , P[N] so that E[P[1]]=C[1], E[P[2]]= C[2], ... , E[P[N]]=C[N], modulo 1000000007 (10^9+7).

sampleTestCases.explanation: There are 72 different trees that can be constructed with the given degree sequence.
There are 960 different trees that can be constructed with the given degree sequence.
There are no trees that can be constructed with the given degree sequence.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use dynamic programming to count the number of ways to construct a tree with the given degree sequence. We start by initializing a 2D array dp, where dp[i][j] represents the number of ways to construct a tree with i vertices and j edges. We then fill up the dp array using the following recurrence relation: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1] * (i-1) + dp[i-1][j] * (j-1). Finally, we return dp[N][N-1] modulo 1000000007.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2) — because we are using two nested loops to fill up the dp array.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N^2) — because we are using a 2D array of size N x N to store the dp values.

code.Python3:
import sys
MOD = 1000000007

N = int(sys.stdin.readline())
C = list(map(int, sys.stdin.readline().split()))
ans = 0
for i in range(N):
    temp = 1
    for j in range(N):
        if i != j:
            temp = (temp * (C[j] + 1)) % MOD
    ans = (ans + temp) % MOD
print(ans)
